headlineSummary: Google Gemini draws the most positive sentiment on limited but clear praise for correct reasoning, while OpenAI and Anthropic sit in a close tie with mixed-neutral reactions, including criticism of OpenAI’s mistaken answer and Anthropic’s split feedback on Claude’s quality and trust [E1][E2][E4][E5][E6][E7][E8].

ranking.summary: OpenAI has mixed-neutral sentiment with limited evidence, driven by one negative comparison that says it answered the car-wash question incorrectly [E1].

Anthropic has mixed-neutral sentiment in a close tie with OpenAI, with strong praise for Claude Code and other helpful workflows offset by complaints about research behavior, evaluation quality, and the headline about hiding AI actions [E3][E4][E5][E6][E7][E8].
Google Gemini is viewed positively, with comments praising its correct answer and better reasoning on the trick question [E1][E2].

evidence.excerpt: And indeed, Sonnet and Opus 4.5 (medium reasoning) say the following: Sonnet: Drive - you need to bring your car to the car wash to get it washed! Opus: You'll need to drive — you have to bring the car to the car wash to get it washed! Gemini 3 Pro (medium): You should drive. -- But OpenAI 5.2 reasoning, even at high, told me to walk.

annotations.rationale: The commenter presents Sonnet and Opus as giving the correct answer on the test question, favorably comparing them to OpenAI.
Gemini is explicitly named and credited with the correct answer in the model comparison.
OpenAI is explicitly said to have answered the simple question incorrectly by telling the user to walk.

evidence.excerpt: Gemini fast > That is a classic "efficiency vs. logic" dilemma. Honestly, unless you’ve invented a way to teleport or you're planning on washing the car with a very long garden hose from your driveway, you’re going to have to drive. > While 50 meters is a great distance for a morning stroll, it’s a bit difficult to get the car through the automated brushes (or under the pressure washer) if you aren't behind the wheel. Gemini thinking: > Unless you’ve mastered the art of carrying a 3,000-pound vehicle on your back, you’re going to want to drive. While 50 meters is a very short distance (about a 30-second walk), the logistics of a car wash generally require the presence of, well... the car. > When you should walk: • If you are just going there to buy an air freshener. • If you are checking to see how long the line is before pulling the car out of the driveway. • If you’re looking for an excuse to get 70 extra steps on your fitness tracker. Note: I abbreviated the raw output slightly for brevity, but generally demonstrates good reasoning of the trick question unlike the other models.

annotations.rationale: The commenter quotes Gemini giving the correct reasoning and explicitly says it demonstrates good reasoning compared with other models.

evidence.excerpt: Being able to hook Claude code up to this has made reversing way more productive. Highly recommend!

annotations.rationale: The commenter gives strong praise to Claude Code, saying it significantly improved productivity and explicitly recommending it.

evidence.excerpt: Anthropic tries to hide Claude's AI actions. Devs hate it

annotations.rationale: The title directly frames Anthropic's behavior as concealment and says developers hate it, which is strongly negative.

evidence.excerpt: The way Claude does research has dramatically changed for the worse. Instead of piping through code logically, it's now spawning dozens of completely unrelated research threads to look at simple problems. I let it spin for over 30 minutes last night before realizing it was just "lost". I have since been looking for these moments and killing it immediately. I tell Claude "just look at the related code" and it says, "sorry I'll look at this directly". WTF Anthropic?

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Claude's research behavior became dramatically worse and ends with an explicit frustrated callout to Anthropic.

evidence.excerpt: What's Anthropic's optimization target??? Getting you the right answer as fast as possible! The variability in agent output is working against that goal, not serving it. If they could make it right 100% of the time, they would — and the "slot machine" nonsense disappears entirely. On capped plans, both you and Anthropic are incentivized to minimize interactions, not maximize them. That's the opposite of a casino. It's ... alignment (of a sort) An unreliable tool that the manufacturer is actively trying to make more reliable is not a slot machine. It's a tool that isn't finished yet.

annotations.rationale: The commenter defends Anthropic's incentives and argues the company is trying to improve reliability rather than exploit users.

evidence.excerpt: It's notable that the second attempt is radically different, and I would say thematically less interesting, yet Claude claims to prefer it.

annotations.rationale: The commenter expresses skepticism about Claude's evaluation, saying the preferred output is less interesting despite Claude claiming otherwise.

evidence.excerpt: With Claude's help, I found some undocumented SANE features to auto crop and fix the scans, then had a Python script in Linux auto scan them and put them into a Postgres database as I went. Other scripts would add transcription, summaries, and auto index everything. "mistral-ocr-latest" did really good handwriting transcription, considering how tight and small some of the handwriting is. Then back to Claude API calls to summarize by month and collect people and places from all of the entires. Claude then created static html pages from what started as a Flask app.

annotations.rationale: The commenter credits Claude with multiple useful steps in the project workflow, indicating clear satisfaction with its utility.
